concept: O(1) slide: add right, remove left
snippet:
nums = [4, 2, 1, 7, 5]
s = nums[0] + nums[1] + nums[2]
s += nums[3] - nums[0]
s
cue: Trace the initial three-term sum, then apply the add-right/remove-left update.
note: Big-O is a rough measure of how the work grows with the input size. O(1) means the work to move the window is constant (one add, one subtract) no matter how long the array is; re-adding every element of the window each step would be O(k) per move.

snippet:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]
k = 3
s = 0
for i in range(k):
    s += nums[i]
s
cue: Add the first k elements the loop visits.

snippet:
nums = [1, 3, 2, 6, 1]
k = 2
s = 0
for i in range(k):
    s += nums[i]
best = s
for i in range(k, len(nums)):
    s += nums[i] - nums[i - k]
    if s > best:
        best = s
best
cue: Sum the first window, then slide and track the running max.

prompt: Write `max_sum_k(nums, k)` returning the largest sum of any contiguous block of exactly `k` elements. Assume 1 <= k <= len(nums). Maintain a running sum with an O(1) slide; do not recompute each block.
cue: Sum the first k elements, then slide: add the entering element, drop the one k back.
note: Sliding the running sum keeps the whole scan O(n); re-summing each block from scratch would be O(n*k).

concept: sum the first window with sum(nums[:k])
cue: Add the elements the slice selects.

prompt: Write `max_average(nums, k)` returning the maximum average of any contiguous block of exactly `k` elements, as a float. Assume 1 <= k <= len(nums). Slide the window in O(1); do not re-sum each block. Using `sum()` once to prime the first window is fine; just do not re-sum every block.
tests:
assert max_average([1, 12, -5, -6, 50, 3], 4) == 12.75, max_average([1, 12, -5, -6, 50, 3], 4)
assert max_average([5], 1) == 5.0, max_average([5], 1)
cue: Track the best size-k sum with an O(1) slide, then convert to an average at the end.

concept: shrink left until the window is valid again
snippet:
nums = [4, 2, 1, 7, 5]
target = 2
left = 0
s = nums[0] + nums[1] + nums[2]
while s > target:
    s -= nums[left]
    left += 1
left
cue: The window sum starts above target. Remove nums[left] and step left forward until the sum drops to target or below.
note: This is just the shrink half of a variable window, on its own. left only ever moves forward, so over the whole scan it advances at most n times total.

snippet:
nums = [2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2]
target = 8
left = 0
s = 0
best = 0
for right in range(len(nums)):
    s += nums[right]
    while s > target:
        s -= nums[left]
        left += 1
    if right - left + 1 > best:
        best = right - left + 1
best
cue: Walk right forward; shrink left while the running sum exceeds target, tracking window length.

prompt: Write `longest_cap(nums, target)` returning the length of the longest contiguous subarray whose sum is at most `target`. All values are non-negative. Use two pointers with an O(1) running sum.
tests:
assert longest_cap([2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2], 8) == 3, longest_cap([2, 1, 5, 1, 3, 2], 8)
assert longest_cap([1, 1, 1], 10) == 3, longest_cap([1, 1, 1], 10)
assert longest_cap([5, 5, 5], 4) == 0, longest_cap([5, 5, 5], 4)
cue: Two pointers: grow right, shrink left while the running sum passes target.

prompt: Write `min_subarray_len(nums, target)` returning the length of the shortest contiguous subarray whose sum is at least `target`, or 0 if none exists. All values are positive. Use two pointers.
tests:
assert min_subarray_len([2, 3, 1, 2, 4, 3], 7) == 2, min_subarray_len([2, 3, 1, 2, 4, 3], 7)
assert min_subarray_len([1, 1, 1, 1], 100) == 0, min_subarray_len([1, 1, 1, 1], 100)
assert min_subarray_len([1, 4, 4], 8) == 2, min_subarray_len([1, 4, 4], 8)
cue: Two pointers: expand right, then shrink left while still at or above target, keeping the min length.
Track each dict count as chars enter, then as 'a' decrements and gets deleted.

concept: longest window with no repeated char
cue: Trace the count-map; shrink left while the just-added char's count is above one.

prompt: Write `length_of_longest_substring(s)` returning the length of the longest substring of `s` with no repeated character. Carry a count-map of the window; grow right and shrink left.
tests:
assert length_of_longest_substring("abcabcbb") == 3, length_of_longest_substring("abcabcbb")
assert length_of_longest_substring("bbbbb") == 1, length_of_longest_substring("bbbbb")
assert length_of_longest_substring("") == 0, length_of_longest_substring("")
assert length_of_longest_substring("pwwkew") == 3, length_of_longest_substring("pwwkew")
cue: Carry a window count-map; grow right, shrink left while the current char repeats.
mapsTo: LC 3 · Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters

cue: Shrink left while the count-map holds more than k keys, tracking the longest span.

concept: longest window with at most k distinct
prompt: Write `longest_at_most_k_distinct(s, k)` returning the length of the longest substring of `s` containing at most `k` distinct characters. Carry a count-map of the window.
tests:
assert longest_at_most_k_distinct("eceba", 2) == 3, longest_at_most_k_distinct("eceba", 2)
assert longest_at_most_k_distinct("aa", 1) == 2, longest_at_most_k_distinct("aa", 1)
assert longest_at_most_k_distinct("", 2) == 0, longest_at_most_k_distinct("", 2)
assert longest_at_most_k_distinct("abaccc", 2) == 4, longest_at_most_k_distinct("abaccc", 2)
cue: Count distinct chars in a window map; shrink left while it holds more than k, deleting keys at zero.
mapsTo: LC 340 · Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters

snippet:
from collections import Counter
Counter("cba") == Counter("abc")
cue: Compare the two as multisets, ignoring order.

prompt: Write `find_anagrams(s, p)` returning a list of the start indices of every substring of `s` that is an anagram of `p`, in increasing order. Slide a fixed window of size len(p), updating a count-map in O(1) per step.
tests:
assert find_anagrams("cbaebabacd", "abc") == [0, 6], find_anagrams("cbaebabacd", "abc")
assert find_anagrams("abab", "ab") == [0, 1, 2], find_anagrams("abab", "ab")
assert find_anagrams("a", "ab") == [], find_anagrams("a", "ab")
cue: Slide a fixed len(p) window, comparing its counts against p's at each step.
mapsTo: LC 438 · Find All Anagrams in a String

concept: missing counter: only still-needed chars decrement it
cue: missing counts required chars not yet covered; it drops only when the entering char is one still needed (its need is positive). A surplus char pushes its need below zero and leaves missing alone.
note: One integer replaces a full count-map comparison: missing == 0 means every required char is covered, in O(1) per step.

prompt: Write `min_window(s, t)` returning the shortest substring of `s` that contains every character of `t` including multiplicity, or "" if none exists. Use a sliding window; keep each slide O(1).
tests:
assert min_window("ADOBECODEBANC", "ABC") == "BANC", min_window("ADOBECODEBANC", "ABC")
assert min_window("a", "a") == "a", min_window("a", "a")
assert min_window("a", "aa") == "", min_window("a", "aa")
assert min_window("a", "b") == "", min_window("a", "b")
cue: Expand right until every needed char is covered, then shrink left to the shortest cover.
note: The missing counter reduces 'is t fully covered?' to an O(1) test each step, instead of comparing whole count-maps.
